---
title: "About this guide"
toc: true
---

## Purpose

This guide supports annotation of images from **Planktivore**, an experimental
passive plankton imager developed at MBARI and deployed on fixed platforms and on
Long-Range AUVs. The instrument carries two optical sets — a **high-magnification**
and a **low-magnification** system. Images are classified by an image classifier
trained on a human-annotated set, so the quality and consistency of human labels
directly determines classifier performance.

The guide has three jobs:

1. **Consistency** — give every annotator the same reference so the same organism
   gets the same label.
2. **Validation** — provide a standard to check existing labels against.
3. **Training** — bring new annotators up to speed quickly.

## How to read a class page

Each class page has the same structure:

- **Defining characteristics** — the features that place an image in this class.
- **Distinguishing from similar classes** — the look-alikes and how to separate them.
  This is the single most important section for consistency.
- **Ideal images** — clean, textbook examples of what the class *should* look like.
- **Challenging images** — messy, partial, or low-contrast frames like those you will
  actually encounter, shown so you can recognise the class in realistic conditions.
- **References** — external keys and resources.

Click any image to open a zoomable view.

## Labelling conventions

- Label to the level the guide defines (usually **genus** for these diatoms); do not
  guess species unless a project-specific key is provided.
- When an image is too degraded to assign confidently, follow the project's
  unknown/low-quality labelling convention rather than forcing a class.
- The **high-mag** and **low-mag** optics can show the same organism very differently;
  each class page notes which optics it applies to.

## Contributing / updating

Content lives in `classes/<class>/meta.yml` plus `ideal/` and `challenging/` image
folders. Edit the metadata or drop in images, run the build, and the HTML site, PDF,
and slide deck all regenerate. See the repository `README.md` for the workflow.
